children.value:
The height of a ball rolling along a marble track -- how can we describe this
mathematically? This seemingly simple question leads us directly to one of the
most important concepts in mathematics: the concept of a function. In this
section we develop the necessary mathematical tools using a marble track as our
guiding example.
You are familiar with the three representations of a function:

Imagine we have set up a simple marble track. There is a starting block, a
straight inclined rail, and a finishing block that acts as a catch container.
Our central question is:

How high is the ball at every position along the
track?

children.alt:
A straight inclined marble track.
(Source: own figure; licence CC BY-SA
4.0)

children.value:
A straight inclined marble track.
(Source: own figure; licence

To describe the height mathematically, we need a coordinate system. We choose
the straight line through the starting block and the finishing block as the
first axis. This line lies on the table surface. As the second axis we choose
the height above the table surface, pointing vertically upward. We are free to
place the origin wherever we like -- we choose the position of the starting
block on the table surface as the origin

children.html: OOO

children.value:
With this coordinate system we can describe the position of the ball precisely.
We measure its distance from the origin along the first axis and call this
position

children.html: xxx

children.value: . We call the height above the table surface

children.html: hhh

children.value: Now we can assign to every position

children.html: xxx
hhh

children.value:
is crucial here: there is no position along the track where the
ball could be at two different heights at the same time. We can measure the
height at several positions and record the results in a table:

children.html: xxx
hhh

children.value:
We have not measured the height at every possible position, but we could.
Even at intermediate positions such as

children.html: x=0.567 cmx = 0.567~\text{cm}x=0.567 cm
x=17.9 cmx = 17.9~\text{cm}x=17.9 cm

children.value: we could measure the corresponding height.

This unique assignment between position and height is the essence of a
mathematical function. In mathematics, such a unique assignment between two
sets is called a
. We define it formally as follows:

is a unique assignment between two sets. Each element of the
first set (the domain) is assigned exactly one element of the second set (the
codomain).
If we abbreviate the domain as

children.html: DDD
WWW
fff

children.value: , we can write this definition compactly as:

children.html: f:D→W,x↦f(x).f: D \to W, \quad x \mapsto f(x).f:D→W,x↦f(x).

children.value: The element of the codomain that is assigned to the element

children.html: xxx

children.value:
of the domain
is called the

children.html: f(x)f(x)f(x)

children.value: How can we represent a function?

children.label: How can we represent a function?

children.value:
For our marble track height function there are several ways of giving a
mathematical description. Each has its own advantages.

. It is particularly
useful when the function is determined by measurement. We have already created
such a table above with our marble track measurements. A table of values is
ideal for experimental data.

In the case of our marble track we can also describe the height of the ball
mathematically in a compact and exact form:

children.html: h(x)=−14x+6.h(x) = -\frac{1}{4} x + 6.h(x)=−41​x+6.
h(x)h(x)h(x)
xxx

children.value:
, measured in centimetres. We now
have an exact formula that allows us to calculate the height at any position.
This representation is called the

. It is precise, compact,
and ideal for further calculations.

. The function
graph corresponds to the side view of our marble track. For each

children.html: xxx

children.value:
-value we
compute the corresponding function value

children.html: h(x)h(x)h(x)
yyy
(x,y)(x, y)(x,y)
xyxyxy

children.value:
Graph of the height function of the ball on the marble track.

The graph makes the behaviour of the function immediately visible and is
particularly useful for building intuition. All three representations describe
the same function — just in different ways.

children.title: CSCA · Math  Section 9·the concept of function

children.value: Video “Math Section 10·representation of function” by CSCA

Our marble track is not infinitely long, and the possible height values are
limited. These physical constraints lead us to two important mathematical
concepts.

children.html: xxx

children.value:
are meaningful. Positions to the left of the starting
block (

children.html: x<0 cmx < 0~\text{cm}x<0 cm

children.value:
) and to the right of the finishing block
(

children.html: x>24 cmx > 24~\text{cm}x>24 cm

children.value: ) are not relevant. We therefore consider only the interval:

children.html: X=[0,24]measured in cm.X = [0, 24] \quad \text{measured in cm.}X=[0,24]measured in cm.

children.value: The set of all valid input values is called the

For the height values we use real numbers. So the possible output of the
function is the so-called

children.html: Y=RY = \mathbb{R}Y=R

children.value:
. But we notice that the
ball starts at

children.html: 6 cm6~\text{cm}6 cm
0 cm0~\text{cm}0 cm

children.value:
. So the height function
takes values in the interval

children.html: f(X)=[0,6]measured in cm,f(X) = [0, 6] \quad \text{measured in cm},f(X)=[0,6]measured in cm,
fff

children.value: What is ... the domain, codomain, and range?

children.html: XXX

children.value: The set of all valid input values (

children.html: xxx

children.value:
-values) for which the
function is defined.

children.html: YYY

children.value:
The set into which the function maps. It contains all
possible output values, but not every value in

children.html: YYY
f(X)f(X)f(X)

children.value:
The set of all output values that the function
actually takes. For our marble track, we have

children.html: f(X)=[0,6]f(X) = [0, 6]f(X)=[0,6]

children.value:
of the function. Outside the domain
our formula

children.html: h(x)=−14x+6h(x) = -\frac{1}{4}x + 6h(x)=−41​x+6

children.value:
produces a number, but it has no
physical meaning — at

children.html: x=−10 cmx = -10~\text{cm}x=−10 cm

children.value: the formula would give a height of

children.html: 8.5 cm8.5~\text{cm}8.5 cm

children.value:
, but there is no rail there. Extrapolation, that is, applying
a formula outside its domain, is a common source of errors in engineering: a
formula is only valid within its domain.

The image tells us which heights the ball can actually reach on our track. This
matters in practice: where does the catch container need to be placed? Could we
extend the range of heights by redesigning the track?
Video “Domain, codomain and range of a function” by MatheMagician

children.title: Domain, codomain and range of a function

children.value: Video “Domain, Codomain, and Range” by Center of Math

Using a simple marble track we have developed the foundations of functions: a
function is a unique assignment between an input value and an output value,
which we can represent as a table of values, a function formula, or a function
graph. The domain and image define the validity range and the set of reachable
values of our mathematical description. Our straight inclined rail is only the
beginning. In the next section we extend the marble track system to discover
important properties that functions can have.
